definition: Routine daily activities that a person performs independently, including bathing, dressing, grooming, toileting, eating, and transferring.
Movement of a limb away from the midline of the body.
Movement of a limb toward the midline of the body.
The act of walking or moving from place to place.
The heartbeat heard at the apex of the heart using a stethoscope, located at the 5th intercostal space at the midclavicular line.
The absence of disease-causing microorganisms. Medical asepsis reduces the number of microorganisms; surgical asepsis eliminates all microorganisms.
The inhalation of food, fluid, or foreign material into the airway, which can cause choking or pneumonia.
The force exerted by blood against the walls of the arteries. Recorded as systolic (contraction) over diastolic (relaxation) pressure. Normal adult range: 120/80 mmHg.
The use of correct posture, balance, and movement to prevent injury when lifting, moving, or repositioning patients.
A flexible tube inserted into the body to drain fluids; a urinary (Foley) catheter drains urine from the bladder.
Permanent shortening and tightening of muscles or tendons causing deformity and limited range of motion, often resulting from prolonged immobility.
Also known as a pressure ulcer or bedsore; a wound caused by prolonged pressure on skin over bony prominences.
Difficulty breathing or shortness of breath.
Swelling caused by excess fluid accumulation in body tissues.
The ability to understand and share the feelings of another person; different from sympathy in that it involves perspective-taking.
Straightening of a joint, increasing the angle between bones.
Bending of a joint, decreasing the angle between bones.
A semi-sitting position where the head of the bed is raised 45–60 degrees. Semi-Fowler's is 30–45 degrees. Used to aid breathing and feeding.
A belt used to help transfer or ambulate a patient safely by providing a secure grip around the patient's waist.
The most important method of preventing the spread of infection; includes handwashing with soap and water or using alcohol-based hand sanitizer.
An emergency technique used to dislodge an object from the airway of a choking person using abdominal thrusts.
High blood pressure; a systolic reading consistently above 140 mmHg or diastolic above 90 mmHg.
Low blood pressure; systolic reading below 90 mmHg. Orthostatic hypotension occurs when blood pressure drops upon standing.
The inability to control bladder (urinary) or bowel (fecal) function.
Measurement and recording of all fluids taken in (oral, IV) and all fluids expelled (urine, drainage) to monitor fluid balance.
Practices used to prevent the spread of infection, including standard precautions, contact, droplet, and airborne precautions.
Side-lying position; used to relieve pressure on the back and sacrum.
A technique used to turn a patient as a unit, maintaining spinal alignment; used for patients with spinal injuries.
Failure to provide the standard of care that a reasonably prudent person would provide, resulting in harm to a patient.
Communication without words, including body language, facial expressions, eye contact, gestures, and touch.
Latin for 'nothing by mouth' (nil per os); a patient restriction from eating or drinking, often before surgery or procedures.
A surgically created opening (stoma) in the body to divert waste. A colostomy diverts stool; a urostomy diverts urine.
A microorganism that causes disease, including bacteria, viruses, fungi, and parasites.
Equipment worn to minimize exposure to hazards, including gloves, gowns, masks, goggles, and face shields.
Lying face down on the abdomen.
A non-invasive measurement of oxygen saturation in the blood (SpO2). Normal range: 95–100%.
The full movement potential of a joint. Active ROM is performed by the patient; passive ROM is performed by the CNA.
The process of breathing; one respiration = one inhalation + one exhalation. Normal adult rate: 12–20 breaths/minute.
Devices used to limit patient movement; require a physician order, patient/family consent, and regular monitoring. The least restrictive method must always be tried first.
The procedures, actions, and processes a healthcare worker is permitted to perform based on their training, experience, and licensure.
Lying flat on the back (face up).
A vital sign measuring body heat. Normal oral temperature: 98.6°F (37°C). Can be measured orally, rectally, axillary, or tympanically.
See Gait Belt; a safety device used when assisting patients with movement.
Position where the body is laid flat on the back with the feet elevated higher than the head; used in some emergency situations.
An infection in any part of the urinary system. Common in catheterized patients; symptoms include burning, frequency, and cloudy urine.
